title: EXPOSÉ VII ÉTALE SITE AND TOPOS OF A SCHEME

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VII ÉTALE SITE AND TOPOS OF A SCHEME

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII
ÉTALE SITE AND TOPOS OF A SCHEMEby A. Grothendieck
Introduction

In the present exposé and the next, we develop the most elementary properties concerning the étale topology and cohomology. The developments of the present exposé concern certain properties valid essentially for other, very different topologies, such as the "fppf topology". In the following exposé we shall develop properties rather special to the étale topology, arising from the very particular nature of étale morphisms.
We follow here in part three oral lectures of J.E. ROOS (which he had been unable to write up), notably in the proof of VIII 6.3.
Unless expressly stated otherwise, it will be understood that the schemes considered in the present exposé and the following ones are elements of the fixed universe \(\mathcal{U}\).
1. The étale topology

We shall denote by (Sch) the category of schemes (elements of the fixed Universe \(\mathcal{U}\)). Recall that a morphism \(f : X \to Y\) in (Sch) is said to be étale if it is locally of finite presentation, flat, and if for every \(y \in Y\), the fiber \(X_y\) is discrete, and its local rings are finite separable extensions of \(k(y)\). It amounts to the same to say that \(f\) is locally of finite presentation, and that for every \(Y\)-scheme \(Y'\) which is affine (in the absolute sense) and every subscheme \(Y'_0\) defined by a nilpotent Ideal, the map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_Y(Y', X) \to \mathrm{Hom}_Y(Y'_0, X) \] is bijective. For the most important properties of this notion, I refer to EGA IV §§ 17 and 18, and meanwhile to SGA 1 I and IV.

One calls étale topology on (Sch) the topology generated by the pretopology for which, for every \(X \in (\mathrm{Sch})\), the set Cov \(X\) consists of the families \((X_i \xrightarrow{u_i} X)_{i \in I}\) (indexed by an \(I \in \mathcal{U}\)) such that the \(u_i\) are étale and \(X = \bigcup_i u_i(X_i)\) (in the set-theoretic sense).

It is convenient to associate with every \(X\) the full subcategory \(\mathrm{Et}/X\) of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) consisting of the arrows \(X' \to X\) which are étale. We shall equip it with the "induced topology" (III) coming from the étale topology of (Sch), (also called the "étale topology on \(X\)") and we shall denote by \(X_{\text{ét}}\) the site thus obtained (the "étale site" of \(X\))(∗). Note that every morphism of \(\mathrm{Et}/X\) is étale (SGA 1 I 4.8), whence it follows that a family \((X'_i \xrightarrow{u_i} X')_{i \in I}\) in \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is covering if and only if it is surjective, i.e. \(\bigcup_i u_i(X'_i) = X'\). One sees at once (cf. 3.1) that \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, hence the results of Exp. I to VI are applicable. The topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is the étale topos of \(X\).

In what follows, unless expressly stated otherwise, all the topological notions that we shall consider in \(\mathrm{Et}/X\) or (Sch) are understood in the sense of the étale topology. Moreover, in the language and the notation, one will commonly write \(X\) instead of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) or \(\mathrm{Et}/X\). Thus, one will call "sheaf on \(X\)" (understood: for the étale topology) a sheaf on the site \(X_{\text{ét}}\). One will denote by \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) the category of these sheaves, which is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. If \(F\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X\), one will denote by \(H^i(X,F)\) its cohomology groups, which would be denoted \(H^i(X_{\text{ét}},F)\) in Exp. V.

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of schemes. Then the inverse image functor \[ f^* : Y' \mapsto Y' \times_Y X \] induces a functor \[ (*) \qquad f^*_{\text{ét}} : \mathrm{Et}/Y \to \mathrm{Et}/X \] which commutes with finite \(\varprojlim\) and transforms covering families into covering families; it is consequently a morphism of sites \[ f^*_{\text{ét}} : X_{\text{ét}} \to Y_{\text{ét}} , \] thus inducing a functor on the category of sheaves: \[ f_*^{\text{ét}} : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}} \] by \[ f_*^{\text{ét}}(F) = F \circ f^*_{\text{ét}} . \] Moreover \(f_*^{\text{ét}}\) admits a left adjoint \[ f_{\text{ét}}^* : \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] extending the one considered in (*), and commuting with arbitrary \(\varinjlim\) and finite \(\varprojlim\), i.e. \(f_{\text{ét}}^*\) defines a morphism of topoi \[ f_{\text{ét}} : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}} \] . Evidently, for a composite of two morphisms of schemes \[ X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} Z \] one has canonical isomorphisms \[ \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} (gf)_*^{\text{ét}} \simeq g_*^{\text{ét}} f_*^{\text{ét}} , & \text{whence} \\[0.6ex] (gf)_{\text{ét}}^* \simeq f_{\text{ét}}^* g_{\text{ét}}^* , & \text{i.e. one has an isomorphism} \\[0.6ex] (gf)_{\text{ét}} \simeq g_{\text{ét}} f_{\text{ét}} , \end{array} \right. \] (so that one obtains a "pseudo-functor" (SGA 1 VI 8) from the category (Sch) into the category \((\mathrm{Top})\) of topoi \(\in \mathcal{U}'\), where \(\mathcal{U}'\) is the smallest universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{U}'\) ; compare IV ).
1.5. Notations

In what follows we shall often write \(f^*\), \(f_*\) instead of \(f^*_{\text{ét}}\), \(f_*^{\text{ét}}\). If \(f : X \to Y\) is a morphism of schemes, the functors \(R^i f_*^{\text{ét}}\) will thus be simply denoted \(R^i f_*\). Let us recall, with this notation, the Leray spectral sequence for \(f\) and an abelian sheaf \(F\) on \(X\) (V 5): \[ H^*(X,F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = H^p(Y, R^q f_*(F)). \] Likewise, if one has two morphisms \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\), one has a Leray spectral sequence (spectral sequence of composite functors) \[ R^*(gf)_*(F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{p,q} = R^p g_*(R^q f_*(F)) . \]

When \(f : X \to Y\) is itself an étale morphism, one may consider \(X\) as an object of \(Y_{\text{ét}}\), and one has a canonical isomorphism of sites \[ X_{\text{ét}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (Y_{\text{ét}})_{/X} . \] By means of this isomorphism, the functor \(f^*_{\text{ét}} : Y' \mapsto Y' \times_Y X\) of (*) is identified with the internal base-change functor in the category \(\mathrm{Et}/Y\). It follows that the functor \[ f^* : \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] is then isomorphic to the "restriction to \(Y\)" functor \[ f^*(F) \simeq F \circ i_{X/Y} , \] where \(F\) is a sheaf on \(Y_{\text{ét}}\), and \(i_{X/Y} : X_{\text{ét}} \to Y_{\text{ét}}\) is the obvious functor (consisting in regarding a scheme étale over \(X\), \(u : X' \to X\), as a scheme étale over \(Y\) by \(fu : X' \to Y\)).
1.7. Questions of Universes

One will note that if \(X \neq \varnothing\), then \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is not an element of the chosen universe \(\mathcal{U}\). However, as we have pointed out, one sees easily (3.1) that one can find a full subcategory \(C\) of \(X_{\text{ét}}\), an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), satisfying the conditions of the "comparison lemma" (III), so that the restriction functor induces an equivalence \(\widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\). Thus, the étale topos is equivalent to a topos defined in terms of a site \(C \in \mathcal{U}\). In other words, \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, hence by virtue of loc. cit. the results of Exposés I to VI are applicable to this site.

footnotes.html: (∗) Note however that it would be preferable to denote by \(X_{\text{ét}}\) the topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) defined by the étale site of \(X\). For practical reasons, we shall keep in this Seminar to the notation introduced here (which is that of the original seminar).

blocks.html: 2. Examples of sheaves

a) Let \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\, (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) be a scheme over \(X\), and for every \(X'\) over \(X\), set \[ F(X') = \mathrm{Hom}_X(X',F) . \] The functor \((\mathrm{Sch})^\circ_{/X} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) thus defined is a sheaf for the étale topology (and even for the finer fpqc topology studied in SGA 3 IV). In other words the étale topology on (Sch) is coarser than the canonical topology. This is indeed, essentially, the content of SGA 1 VIII 5.1 (cf. also SGA 3 IV 6.3.1). A fortiori, the restriction of this sheaf to \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is a sheaf. We shall again denote it by \(F\), when no confusion is to be feared(∗). Note also that the functor thus obtained \[ (*) \qquad (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] commutes with finite \(\varprojlim\) (this is trivial). This implies for example that when \(F\) is a group scheme (resp...) over \(X\), then the sheaf it defines is a sheaf of groups (resp...). Note that the functor \(\mathrm{Et}/X \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) induced by (*) is none other than the canonical functor, associating with every \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) the functor on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) that it represents. It is thus an isomorphism of the category \(\mathrm{Et}/X\) onto a full subcategory of the étale topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), by means of which we generally identify an \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\) with the corresponding sheaf, which will be denoted \(\widetilde{X}\) or simply \(X\).
Evidently one will have \(H^0(X,F) = \mathrm{Hom}_{(\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}}(X,F)\).

Let us also give an interpretation of \(H^1(X,F)\) when \(F\) is a group prescheme over \(X\) (commutative if one wishes, so that the definition of \(H^1(X,F)\) falls within Exp. V ; for the general case one may consult the thesis of J. Giraud(∗∗). Then the well-known arguments, which I refrain from repeating here, show that \(H^1(X,F)\) is canonically isomorphic to the group of classes (mod. isomorphism) of sheaves of sets on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) that are principal homogeneous under \(F\)(∗∗). When \(F\) is affine over \(X\), then SGA 1 VIII 2.1 implies (again by standard arguments, cf. thesis of J.Giraud) that \(H^1(X,F)\) is also the group of classes of schemes \(P\) over \(X\), on which \(F\) operates on the right and which are "principal homogeneous bundles over \(X\) in the sense of the étale topology", i. e. locally trivial in the sense of the étale topology(∗∗∗).
Remarks 2.1

One will take care that if, for every scheme \(Z\) over \(X\), one denotes by \(\varphi_X(Z)\) the associated sheaf on \(X_{\text{ét}}\), and if \(f : Y \to X\) is a morphism of schemes, one has an obvious homomorphism (functorial in \(Z\)) \[ (*) \qquad f^*(\varphi_X(Z)) \to \varphi_Y(Z_Y) , \qquad Z_Y = Z \times_X Y , \] i.e. an obvious homomorphism \[ \varphi_X(Z) \to f_*(\varphi_Y(Z_Y)) , \] namely the homomorphism functorial in \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\) \[ \mathrm{Hom}_X(X', Z) \to \mathrm{Hom}_Y(X'_Y, Z_Y) . \] But one will take care that in general (*) is not an isomorphism, i.e. the formation of the étale sheaf associated with a relative scheme does not commute with inverse image functors. However (*) is an isomorphism in the particular case where \(Z\) is étale over \(X\).

Likewise, the functor \[ \varphi_X : (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] is not faithful if \(X \neq \varnothing\)(∗) ; however its restriction to \(X_{\text{ét}}\), which is none other than the canonical functor \(X_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), is fully faithful, since by virtue of a) the topology of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is coarser than its canonical topology.

b) Note that (*) also commutes with direct sums indexed by the \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), as one verifies easily. In particular, if for every set \(I\) one denotes by \(I_X\) the constant \(X\)-scheme defined by \(I\) (SGA 3 I 1.8), the direct sum of \(I\) copies of \(X\), then the associated sheaf is none other than the constant sheaf \(I_{X_{\text{ét}}}\) (direct sum of \(I\) copies of the final sheaf on \(X_{\text{ét}}\)). Since the functor \(I \mapsto I_X\) also commutes with finite \(\varprojlim\), one sees that it transforms group into group, commutative group into commutative group, etc... If \(G\) is an ordinary commutative group, one will simply write \(H^1(X,G)\) instead of \(H^1(X,G_X)\). Suppose, for example, that \(G\) is a finite group; then \(G_X\) is finite, hence affine over \(X\), and so using the final remark of a) one obtains an interpretation of \(H^1(X,G)\) as the set of classes of principal Galois coverings with group \(G\) (SGA 1 V 2.7). When \(X\) is connected and equipped with a geometric point \(a\), then in terms of the "fundamental group" \(\pi_1(X,a)\) (SGA 1 V 7) one obtains the canonical isomorphism \[ (**) \qquad H^1(X,G) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(\pi_1(X, a), G), \] (where \(G\) has been assumed commutative).

One may say that in passing from Zariski cohomology to the étale topology, "one has done what was needed" to obtain "the correct" \(H^1\) (which appears on the right-hand side of (**) for a finite constant coefficient group \(G\). It is a remarkable fact, which will be proved in the sequel of this seminar, that this also suffices to find the "correct" \(H^i(X,G)\) for every torsion coefficient group (at least if \(G\) is prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\)).

c) Let \(F\) be an \(O_X\)-Module on \(X\), in the sense of the Zariski topology. Then (with the notation of SGA 3 I 4.6) one defines a functor \[ W(F) : (\mathrm{Sch})^\circ_{/X} \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] by \[ W(F)(X') = \Gamma(X', F \otimes_{O_X} O_{X'}) . \] This functor is again a sheaf for the étale topology (and even for the fpqc topology), as again follows from SGA 1 VIII 1.6 and SGA 3 IV 6.3.1. A fortiori, the restriction of \(W(F)\) to \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is again a sheaf, which one will again denote \(W(F)\). By definition one will thus have \(H^0(X,W(F)) = \Gamma(F) = H^0(X,F)\). But one has better if \(F\) is quasi-coherent, cf. 4.3.

footnotes.html: (∗) But one will take care that if \(X \neq \varnothing\), the functor \(\varphi\) which associates with \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) the corresponding sheaf \(\varphi(F)\) is not fully faithful, nor even faithful, and that one cannot reconstitute \(F\) (mod. isom.) knowing \(\varphi(F)\). For example; if \(S = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(k\) an algebraically closed field, knowledge of \(\varphi(F)\) is equivalent to that of the underlying set of \(F\) only (by virtue of VIII 2.4). Compare IV
(∗∗) or \(F\)-torsors in the terminology of loc.cit.
(∗∗∗) or \(F\)-representable torsors in the now-accepted terminology.
(∗) Cf. for this the footnote at the bottom of page 6.

pages.title: 3. Generators of the Étale Topos. Cohomology of a \(\varinjlim\) of sheaves

blocks.html: 3. Generators of the Étale Topos. Cohomology of a \(\varinjlim\) of sheaves

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(C\) a full subcategory of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) such that for every \(X'\) étale over \(X\) which is affine, \(C\) contains an object isomorphic to \(X'\). Then \(C\) is a "family of topological generators" of the site \(X_{\text{ét}}\), hence a family of generators of the topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), and the restriction functor \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{C}\) is an equivalence of categories (where \(C\) is equipped with the topology induced by that of \(X_{\text{ét}}\)).
Trivial by means of the "comparison lemma".

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Suppose that \(X\) is quasi-separated. Let us call the restricted étale site of \(X\) the full subcategory \(C\) of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) consisting of the schemes étale over \(X\) which are of finite presentation over \(X\), equipped with the topology induced by \(X_{\text{ét}}\). Then:

  1. (i)

    \(C\) is stable under fibered products, and is a site of finite type if \(X\) is quasi-compact

  2. (ii)

    The restriction functor \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{C}\) is an equivalence of categories.

Assertion (i) is trivial, and (ii) follows from 3.1 because \(C\) satisfies the condition of 3.1 thanks to the fact that \(X\) is quasi-separated, which implies that if \(X'\) is quasi-compact, for example affine, it is quasi-compact over \(X\) (EGA IV 1.2.4, where one takes \(Z = \operatorname{Spec} \mathbf{Z}\)), hence of finite presentation over \(X\) if \(X'\) is locally of finite type over \(X\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

Suppose \(X\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the functors \(H^q(X_{\text{ét}}, F)\) on the category of abelian sheaves on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) commute with \(\varinjlim\).

Indeed, one may replace \(X_{\text{ét}}\) by \(C\) by virtue of 3.2 (ii); now since \(X\) is quasi-compact one has \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), and the conclusion follows from 3.2 (i) and VI § 6 1.2 (3).
4. Comparison with other topologies

First of all, note that the examples of sheaves on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) considered in N° 2 are in fact, in a natural way, restrictions of sheaves defined on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) equipped with its étale topology (or even the fpqc topology). Let, in general, \[ u^* : X_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X} \] be the inclusion functor, which is continuous (III) and commutes with finite \(\varprojlim\), hence defines a morphism of sites: \[ u : (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X} \to X_{\text{ét}} , \] whence a functor \[ u_* : \widetilde{(\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] on the associated categories of sheaves: \[ u_*(F) = F \circ u^* , \] and a left adjoint functor(1) of the latter \[ u^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{(\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}} \] This being posed:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1
  1. (i)

    The functor \(u^*\) is fully faithful, hence for every sheaf \(G\) on \(X_{\text{ét}}\), the canonical homomorphism \(G \mapsto u_* u^*(G)\) is an isomorphism.

  2. (ii)

    Let \(G\) (resp. \(F\)) be an abelian sheaf on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) (resp. \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\)). Then the following canonical homomorphisms (defined for example as edge-homomorphisms of the Leray spectral sequence)

are isomorphisms: \[ H^*(X_{\text{ét}},F \circ u^*) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^*((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}, F) \] \[ H^*(X_{\text{ét}},G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^*((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}, u^*(G)) . \] Indeed, the functor \(u\) is a comorphism and one may then apply (III).
Thus, (i) shows that for the study of the cohomology of sheaves, it is essentially equivalent to work with the "small" étale site \(X_{\text{ét}}\) or the "big" étale site \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\).

On the other hand, it is appropriate to introduce in (Sch) (hence in the \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\)) various topologies other than the étale topology, of which the most useful are defined in SGA 3 IV 6.3. The coarsest among the latter is the Zariski topology (Zar), defined by the pretopology in which the covering families are the surjective families of open immersions; it is coarser than the étale topology. The finest of these topologies is the "faithfully flat quasi-compact" topology, abbreviated (fpqc), which is the coarsest of the topologies for which the covering families in the sense of Zariski, as well as the faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms, are covering; the fpqc topology is finer than the étale topology. As we have already remarked, the various examples of sheaves on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) considered in N° 2 are in fact already sheaves for the fpqc topology.

One will however take care that for an abelian sheaf \(F\) on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) for the (fpqc) topology (or a topology, such as fppf, considered in SGA 3), the cohomology groups of \(F\) for the fpqc topology (resp....) are not always isomorphic to the cohomology groups for the étale topology, and this even if \(X\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(F\) is representable by an algebraic group over \(k\), even as regards \(H^1\). In general, one can show that the étale topology gives the "correct" cohomology groups for coefficient groups which are étale group schemes, or more generally smooth ones, over \(X\)(∗), but it is no longer so for group schemes such as the radicial groups over \(S\), for which it is appropriate to replace the étale topology, still too coarse, by the fpqc topology, or fppf.

As an example of the relations between the cohomologies relative to different topologies, let us point out here the case of the Zariski and étale topologies. We shall denote by \(X_{\mathrm{Zar}}\) the site of Zariski opens of \(X\), so that one has a canonical inclusion functor \[ u^* : X_{\mathrm{Zar}} \to X_{\text{ét}} \] which defines a morphism of sites \[ u : X_{\text{ét}} \to X_{\mathrm{Zar}} , \] whence corresponding functors direct image \[ f_* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\mathrm{Zar}} \qquad (f_*(F) = F \circ u^*) , \] and inverse image \[ f^* : \widetilde{X}_{\mathrm{Zar}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} , \] adjoint to one another; geometrically, it is appropriate to interpret the pair \((f_*, f^*)\) as a morphism of topoi \[ f : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\mathrm{Zar}} . \] From this one deduces a homomorphism of cohomological functors \[ H^*(X_{\mathrm{Zar}}, f_*(F)) \to H^*(X_{\text{ét}},F) \] and a Leray spectral sequence \[ H^*(X_{\text{ét}},F) \Longleftarrow H^p(X_{\mathrm{Zar}},R^q f_*(F)) , \] where \(F\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X_{\text{ét}}\). This spectral sequence summarizes the general relations between étale cohomology and Zariski cohomology. Of course, for coefficient sheaves \(F\) such as constant coefficient sheaves, this spectral sequence is in general far from trivial, i.e. in general one will have \(R^q f_*(F) \neq 0\) (take notably the case where \(X\) is the spectrum of a field). However:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3

Let \(F\) be a variable sheaf of modules on the prescheme \(X\) (a sheaf in the sense of the Zariski topology), whence a sheaf \(F_{\text{ét}}\) on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) (cf. 2, c) and a homomorphism of cohomological functors \[ H^*(X,F) \to H^*(X_{\text{ét}}, F_{\text{ét}}) . \] When \(F\) is quasi-coherent, the preceding homomorphism is an isomorphism.

Indeed, the left-hand side is none other than \(H^*(X_{\mathrm{Zar}}, f_*(F_{\text{ét}}))\), and with the preceding notation, it suffices to prove that one has \[ R^q f_*(F) = 0 \qquad \text{for } q > 0 . \] This follows at once, thanks to the procedure for computing the \(R^q f_*\) and thanks to the fact that the affine opens form a base of the topology of \(X\), from the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

If \(X\) is affine, one has \[ H^q(X_{\text{ét}}, F_{\text{ét}}) = 0 \qquad \text{for } q > 0 . \]

To see this, let \(C = X_{\text{ét}}\), \(C'\) the full subcategory consisting of the affine \(X'\); then by virtue of 3.1 one will have \[ H^q(C,F) \simeq H^q(C',F|C') . \] It thus suffices to prove that \(F|C'\) is \(C'\)-acyclic (V 4.1) or again satisfies the condition of V 4.3, i.e. that for every \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C'\) and every covering family \(R = (X'_i \to X')_i\) in \(C'\), one has \(H^q(R, F) = 0\) for \(q > 0\). Now one may suppose \((X'_i)_i\) finite, then, up to replacing the \(X'_i\) by their sum, that the covering family consists of a single morphism \(X'_i \to X'\) which is covering, i.e. (étale and) surjective. Thus one is reduced to proving that if \(f : X \to Y\) is a surjective étale morphism of affine schemes and \(F\) a quasi-coherent module on \(X\), then \[ (*) \qquad H^q(X/Y,F) = 0 \qquad \text{for } q > 0 . \] Now this is proved in TDTE I, B, 1.1, (in FGA, cf. ref [4] of IX).
Remarks 4.5

In fact, by loc. cit., to have (*) it suffices that \(X \to Y\) be surjective and flat (instead of étale). This allows one to show by the preceding proof that one still has isomorphisms analogous to that of 3.3, replacing therein the étale site \(X_{\text{ét}}\) by \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\), equipped with any of the topologies finer than that of Zariski considered in SGA 3 IV 6.3, for example the fpqc topology.

footnotes.html: (1) Strictly speaking, since \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category but not a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, one cannot invoke III for the existence of \(u^*\) ; however, one easily constructs \(u^*\) by \(u^*(F)(X') = (p_{X'})_{\text{ét}}^*(F)(X')\) (\(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \mathrm{Sch}_{/X}\)), where \(p_{X'}\) denotes the structural morphism \(X' \to X\). On the other hand, to give a meaning to 4.1 (ii) and to justify the indicated proof of 4.1, it is appropriate to introduce a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\), and to consider the sheaves occurring in the formulas 4.1 (i) as sheaves with values in \(\mathcal{V}\)-(Ens). One may also, if one wishes to work only with U-sites, replace \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) by a full subcategory \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) stable under finite \(\varprojlim\), containing \(C\) of 3.1, and which is U-small.
(∗) Cf. A. Grothendieck, Le groupe de Brauer III, th. 11.7, in "Dix exposés sur la topologie des schémas, North Holland Pub. Cie.

pages.title: 5. Cohomology of an inverse limit of schemes

blocks.html: 5. Cohomology of an inverse limit of schemes

Let \(I\) be an increasing filtered preordered set, \[ \mathscr{X} = (X_i)_{i \in I} \] an inverse system of schemes, the transition morphisms \(u_i : X_i \to X_j\) \((i \geq j)\) being affine. Recall (EGA IV 8...) that under these conditions, the inverse limit \[ X = \varprojlim X_i \] exists in the category of schemes (and even in the category of spaces ringed in local rings) and can be constructed as follows: one chooses \(i_0 \in I\), so that for \(i \geq i_0\), one has an \(X_{i_0}\)-isomorphism (EGA II 1.2 and 1.3) \[ X_i = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{A}_i , \] where \(\mathcal{A}_i\) is a quasi-coherent Algebra on \(X_{i_0}\), the \(\mathcal{A}_i\) \((i \geq i_0)\) thus forming a direct system of such Algebras. Setting \[ \mathcal{A} = \varinjlim_i \mathcal{A}_i , \] one may take \[ X = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{A} . \] Moreover, denoting by esp \(S\) the topological space underlying a scheme \(S\), one shows (EGA IV 8...) that the canonical map \[ \mathrm{esp}\, X \to \varprojlim \mathrm{esp}(X_i) \] is a homeomorphism and that the canonical homomorphism of sheaves of rings \[ \varinjlim_i \gamma_i^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_{X_i}) \to \mathcal{O}_X \] is bijective, where \(\gamma_i : X \to X_i\) is the canonical continuous map.

In pictorial terms, one may summarize the content of EGA IV 8,9 by saying that, when \(X_{i_0}\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, then "any datum of schematic nature on \(X\), of finite presentation over \(X\)", is equivalent to a datum of the same nature on one of the \(X_i\), "for \(i\) large enough". Thus, one proves (EGA IV 8...):

a) if \(i_1 \in I\) and if \(X'_{i_1}\), \(X''_{i_1}\) are two schemes of finite presentation over \(X_{i_1}\), then setting, for every \(i \geq i_1\), \[ X'_i = X'_{i_1} \times_{X_{i_1}} X_i , \qquad X' = X'_{i_1} \times_{X_{i_1}} X , \] and defining likewise \(X''_i\), \(X''\), the canonical map \[ \varinjlim_{i \geq i_1} \mathrm{Hom}_{X_i}(X'_i, X''_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}_X(X', X'') \] is bijective.

b) For every scheme \(X'\) of finite presentation over \(X\), there exist an index \(i_1 \in I\), a scheme \(X'_{i_1}\) of finite presentation over \(X_{i_1}\), and an \(X\)-isomorphism \[ X' \simeq X'_{i_1} \times_{X_{i_1}} X . \]

One may express the preceding result, and the results of analogous nature contained in EGA IV 8, in the language of \(\varinjlim\) of fibered categories introduced in VI. For this, let us consider the category \(\mathscr{F}\) of morphisms \(f : T \to S\) of finite presentation of schemes, and the "target functor" \[ \pi : \mathscr{F} \to (\mathrm{Sch}) , \] which is evidently a fibering functor, the fiber categories being moreover equivalent to \(\mathcal{U}\)-small categories. Let us consider the inverse image of the fibered category \(\mathscr{F}/(\mathrm{Sch})\) by the functor

(where \(\mathrm{cat}(I)\) is the category associated with \(I\), with \(\mathrm{Hom}(i,j) \neq \varnothing\) if and only if \(i \geq j\)), whence a fibered category \[ \pi_{\mathscr{X}} : \mathscr{F}_{\mathscr{X}} \to \mathrm{cat}(I) , \] whose fiber at each \(i \in I\) is canonically isomorphic to the full subcategory \(\mathscr{F}_{X_i}\) of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X_i}\) consisting of the schemes of finite presentation over \(X_i\), the base-change morphism \(\mathscr{F}_i \to \mathscr{F}_j\) relative to \(j \geq i\) being none other than \(X'_i \mapsto X'_j = X'_i \times_{X_i} X_j\). This being posed, associating with every \(X'_i\) of finite presentation over \(X_i\) its inverse image over \(X\) \[ \varphi(X'_i) = X'_i \times_{X_i} X , \] one finds a natural functor \[ \varphi : \mathscr{F}_{\mathscr{X}} \to \mathscr{F}_X , \] which evidently transforms a cartesian morphism of \(\mathscr{F}_{\mathscr{X}}\) into an isomorphism, hence by definition factors uniquely through a canonical functor \[ (*) \qquad \psi : \varinjlim_{\mathscr{F}_{\mathscr{X}}/\mathrm{cat}\, I} \mathscr{F}_{\mathscr{X}} \to \mathscr{F}_X . \] Taking into account the description of the left-hand side, the result of EGA IV 8 recalled above may then be stated by saying that (*) is an equivalence of categories.

Statement a) of 5.2 above can be made precise in various ways, by introducing some set \((\mathcal{M})\) of morphisms of schemes, stable under base change, and by stating that for a given \(X_{i_1}\)-morphism \(u_{i_1} : X'_{i_1} \to X''_{i_1}\), the corresponding morphism \(u' : X' \to X''\) is \(\in (\mathcal{M})\) if and only if there exists \(i \geq i_1\) such that the \(X_i\)-morphism \(u_i : X'_i \to X''_i\) is \(\in (\mathcal{M})\). The statement thus obtained is true, for example, when \((\mathcal{M})\) is one of the following sets of arrows: proper morphisms (respectively projective, resp. quasi-projective, resp. affine, resp. quasi-affine, resp. finite, resp. quasi-finite, resp. radicial, resp. surjective, resp. flat, resp. smooth, resp. étale, resp. unramified). The reader will find the corresponding statements in EGA IV par. 8 (for the first 9), par. 11 (for the flat case), par. 17 (for the last three cases).

Let us then replace \(\mathscr{F} \to (\mathrm{Sch})\) by the fibered subcategory \(\mathscr{G}\) consisting of the étale morphisms of finite presentation, whose fiber category \(\mathscr{G}_S\), for every scheme \(S\), is none other than the category of schemes étale of finite presentation over \(S\), and let us likewise form the fibered category \(\mathscr{G}_{\mathscr{X}}\) over \(\mathrm{cat}(I)\), whose fiber category at every \(i \in I\) is the category \(\mathscr{G}_{X_i}\). By virtue of 3.2 (ii), the étale topoi \(\widetilde{X}_{i,\text{ét}}\), \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) are also canonically equivalent to \(\widetilde{\mathscr{G}}_{X_i}\), \(\widetilde{\mathscr{G}}_X\), (where each \(\mathscr{G}_S\) is equipped with the topology induced by \(S_{\text{ét}}\)). Moreover, by 3.2 (i), each \(\mathscr{G}_{X_i}\) is a site of finite type, moreover equivalent to a site \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. Taking into account the topologies on the \(\mathscr{G}_{X_i}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) becomes a fibered site over \(\mathrm{cat}(I)\). This being posed, one may state the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.6

The canonical functor \[ \varinjlim_{\mathscr{G}_{\mathscr{X}}/\mathrm{cat}(I)} \mathscr{G}_{\mathscr{X}} \to \mathscr{G}_X \] defines an equivalence, respecting the topologies, from the site \(\varinjlim\) of the restricted étale sites (3.2) of the \(X_i\) to the restricted étale site of \(X = \varprojlim_i X_i\).

The fact that one obtains an equivalence of categories is one of the statements recalled in 5.4 (the one where \((\mathcal{M})\) is the set of morphisms étale in (Sch)). The assertion concerning the topologies is obtained likewise by taking \((\mathcal{M})\) = set of surjective morphisms in (Sch).
The preceding result allows us to apply the results of to the present situation. One finds in particular

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.7

Let \(\mathscr{X} = (X_i)_{i \in I}\) be an inverse system of schemes, with \(I\) an increasing filtered preordered set. Suppose that the transition morphisms \(u_{ij} : X_j \to X_i\) are affine (so that the scheme \(X = \varprojlim X_i\) is defined (5.1)), and that the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Consider the fibered site \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(\mathrm{cat}(I)\) made explicit in 5.5, whose fiber \(\mathscr{G}_i\) at \(i \in I\) is canonically isomorphic to the "restricted étale site" of \(X_i\) (consisting of the schemes étale of finite presentation over \(X_i\)). Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(\mathscr{G}\) (i.e. (3) a functor \(\mathscr{G}^\circ \to (\mathrm{Ab})\) whose restriction \(F_i\) to each \(\mathscr{G}_i\) is a sheaf, i.e. a sheaf on \(X_i\)). Let \(F_\infty = \varinjlim_i u_i^*(F_i)\) be the sheaf induced on \(X\), where \(u_i : X \to X_i\) is the canonical morphism. Then the canonical homomorphisms \[ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_i,F_i) \to H^n(X,F_\infty) \] are isomorphisms.
Taking 5.6 into account, this follows from the conjunction of VI 7.3 and VI 8.5.2. One will mainly apply 5.7 in the following particular case, also made explicit in

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

With the preceding hypotheses and notation for \((X_i)_{i \in I}\), \(X\), \(u_{ij}\), \(u_i\), let \(i_0 \in I\), and let \(F_{i_0}\) be an abelian sheaf on \(X_{i_0}\). For every \(i \geq i_0\), let \(F_i = u_{i,i_0}^*(F_{i_0})\), and moreover let \(F_\infty = u_{i_0}^*(F_{i_0})\). Under these conditions, the canonical homomorphisms \[ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_i,F_i) \to H^n(X,F_\infty) \] are isomorphisms.
Here, however, is a sometimes useful case which comes under 5.7 and not 5.8:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.9

With the hypotheses and notation of 5.7 for \((X_i)_{i \in I}\), \(X\), \(u_{ij}\), \(u_i\), let \(G_{i_0}\) be a commutative group scheme locally of finite presentation over \(X_{i_0}\) (where \(i_0 \in I\) is given). For every \(i \geq i_0\), let \(G_i = G_{i_0} \times_{X_{i_0}} X_i\), and likewise let \(G_\infty = G_{i_0} \times_{X_{i_0}} X\). Then the canonical homomorphisms \[ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_i,G_i) \to H^n(X,G_\infty) \] are isomorphisms.

Indeed, we know that \(G_{i_0}\) defines a sheaf on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X_{i_0}}\) (cf. 2 (a)), and supposing that \(i_0\) is a least object for \(I\) (which is permissible), whence a canonical functor \(\mathscr{G} \to (\mathrm{Sch})_{/X_{i_0}}\), one finds by composition a contravariant functor \(F\) on \(\mathscr{G}\), whose restriction to \(\mathscr{G}_i\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf on \(X_i\) defined by \(G_i\). In particular \(F\) is a sheaf on \(\mathscr{G}\), and we may apply 5.7 to it. The hypothesis that \(G_{i_0}\) is locally of finite presentation over \(X_{i_0}\) serves to ensure that the sheaf \(F_\infty\) of 5.7 is isomorphic, via the homomorphism natural \(F_\infty \to \mathrm{faisc}(G_\infty)\), to the sheaf \(\mathrm{faisc}(G_\infty)\) defined by \(G_\infty\) (as indeed follows at once from EGA IV 8.8.2 (i), by looking at the values of the sheaves considered on the objects of the restricted étale site of \(G_\infty\) and applying 5.6). This completes the proof of 5.9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

With the hypotheses and notation of 5.7 for \((X_i)_{i \in I}\), \(X\), \(u_{ij}\), \(u_i\), let \(F\) be a sheaf of commutative groups on \(X\); then the canonical homomorphisms \[ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_i,u_{i*}(F)) \to H^n(X,F) \] are isomorphisms.

The statements 5.7 to 5.10 generalize to corresponding statements for the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) of sheaves of Modules, thanks to VI 8.7.9, which we refrain from repeating in the present particular case. We shall, on the other hand, make explicit, for later reference, the variants of 5.8 and 5.9 in terms of the functors \(R^n f_*\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.11

Let \(I\) be an increasing filtered preordered set, \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) and \((Y_i)_{i \in I}\) two inverse systems of schemes, with affine morphisms of transition, \(X = \varprojlim_i X_i\), \(Y = \varprojlim_i Y_i\), \((f_i)_{i \in I}\) an inverse system of quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphisms \(f_i : X_i \to Y_i\), \(i_0 \in I\), \(F_{i_0}\) a sheaf on \(X_{i_0}\). For every \(i \geq i_0\), let \(F_i\) be the sheaf on \(X_i\) inverse image of \(F_{i_0}\). Likewise let \(f: X \to Y\) be deduced from \((f_i)\) and \(F\) the sheaf on \(X\) inverse image of \(F_{i_0}\). Under these conditions, the canonical homomorphism \[ \varinjlim_i u_i^*(R^n f_{i*}(F_i)) \to R^n f_*(F) \] is an isomorphism (where \(u_i : X \to X_i\) is the canonical morphism).

One may suppose that \(i_0\) is a least object of \(I\), and, the question being local on \(Y_{i_0}\), that \(Y_{i_0}\) is affine, so that the \(Y_i\) and the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact and quasi-separated. One is then under the conditions of application of VI 8.7.3 (where one reduces to 5.8, taking into account the computation of the \(R^n f_*\) as sheaves associated with presheaves. One proves likewise:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.12

Same statement as 5.11, except that now \(F_{i_0}\) denotes a commutative group scheme locally of finite presentation over \(X_{i_0}\), and \(F_i,F\) its inverse images (in the sense of base change for schemes).
One will take care that 5.9 (resp. 5.12) is not a particular case of 5.8 (resp. 5.11), cf. 2 a) remark 2.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.13

With the notation of 5.11, let \(F\) be a sheaf of abelian groups on \(X\). Then the canonical homomorphisms \[ \varinjlim_i v_i^*(R^n f_{i*}(u_{i*}(F))) \to R^n f_*(F) \] are isomorphisms, (where \(u_i : X \to X_i\) and \(v_i : Y \to Y_i\) are the canonical homomorphisms).
The proof is essentially the same as before.
Remarks 5.14

a) The preceding passage-to-the-limit results are valid for \(H^0\) resp. the \(f_*\), for sheaves of sets (instead of abelian sheaves), and for \(H^1\) resp. the \(R^1 f_*\) for sheaves of groups (not necessarily commutative), - where the non-commutative \(H^1\) and \(R^1 f_*\) are defined as usual in terms of torsors (or principal homogeneous bundles) (cf. thesis of Giraud(∗)). This can be verified directly in the general context of . It is certainly possible (and doubtless useful) to give also a variant for the non-commutative \(H^2\) of "bands", studied by Giraud.

b) The passage-to-the-limit results for fibered sites developed in VI assumed only that the base category was a filtered category, without its being necessary to suppose that it is associated with a filtered preordered set. The case of an arbitrary filtered index category is likewise the natural framework for the statements developed in the present n°. If we have placed ourselves in too restrictive a framework, this was in order to be able to give correct references to EGA IV, where it is unfortunately assumed in the questions of passage to the limit (starting from par. 8) that the index category is defined in terms of a filtered preordered set. We shall however admit in the sequel that all the results of EGA IV used are valid for arbitrary filtered index categories, (the proofs given in loc. cit. being valid, essentially without change, in this more general case). Accordingly, we shall likewise use without further comment the results of the present n° in the case where \(I\) is replaced by an arbitrary filtered index category.
